questions.question: Article 40.6.1°(i) protects the right of citizens to express freely their convictions and opinions. What limitations does the Constitution expressly impose on this right within the text of Art. 40.6?

options.A: Freedom of expression may be restricted only by an Act of the Oireachtas approved by referendum
options.B: The right is subject to the requirements of public order, morality, and the authority of the State; education, blasphemy, sedition and indecency may be made criminal by law
options.C: Freedom of expression is an absolute right — no constitutional restriction is permissible
options.D: The right may only be restricted where national security is directly threatened, subject to judicial approval

questions.explanation: Article 40.6.1°(i) itself contains express limitations: the education of public opinion is a matter of grave import to the common good, and the State shall endeavour to ensure that organs of public opinion shall not be used to undermine public order or morality or the authority of the State. Publication or utterance of blasphemous, seditious or indecent matter is a criminal offence under the Constitution. These limitations are built into the constitutional text, making freedom of expression in Ireland more explicitly limited than the equivalent provision in some other constitutional systems.

questions.question: Article 40.6.1°(i) specifically references that the State shall endeavour to ensure that organs of public opinion (including the press, radio and cinema) shall not be used to undermine the authority of the State. Does this mean the State may censor the press?

options.A: Yes — the Constitution expressly authorises State censorship of organs of public opinion
options.B: No — the provision is an aspiration, not an authorisation for prior restraint; any restriction on the press must pass constitutional scrutiny including proportionality
options.C: Yes, but only in respect of political commentary that is critical of Government policy
options.D: No, because the ECHR Act 2003 overrides any such censorship power with Art. 10 ECHR freedom of expression

questions.explanation: While Art. 40.6.1°(i) references the State's duty regarding organs of public opinion, this is not a blank authority for prior restraint or censorship. The Supreme Court has consistently held that restrictions on freedom of expression must be proportionate and constitutionally justified. Prior restraints (injunctions against publication) are viewed with particular scepticism: see Mahon v Post Publications Ltd [2007] IESC 15 where the court affirmed that freedom of expression is a fundamental right that can only be restricted by sufficiently compelling reasons.

questions.question: In Murphy v IRTC [1999] 1 IR 12, the Supreme Court upheld a statutory ban on religious advertising on radio and television under s.10(3) of the Radio and Television Act 1988. The court balanced freedom of expression against what competing constitutional value?

options.A: The right to property of broadcasters who would be commercially harmed by religious advertising
options.B: The risk that paid religious broadcasting could undermine the constitutional guarantee of freedom of religion and the constitutional prohibition on State endowment of religion under Art. 44
options.C: National security concerns about foreign religious organisations broadcasting in Ireland
options.D: The right to education of children who could be exposed to religious messages without parental consent

questions.explanation: In Murphy v IRTC [1999] 1 IR 12, the Supreme Court upheld the statutory ban on paid religious advertising on broadcast media on the basis that the restriction was a proportionate means of protecting the constitutionally guaranteed freedom of conscience and freedom of religion under Art. 44, and of preserving neutrality in religious matters on public media. The potential for wealthy religious groups to dominate airtime justified the legislative restriction on this form of expression.

questions.question: The tort of defamation under the Defamation Act 2009 restricts freedom of expression by protecting reputation. How has the Supreme Court balanced freedom of expression against reputation in Irish law?

options.A: Reputation always prevails over freedom of expression; defamation law cannot be challenged on constitutional grounds
options.B: Freedom of expression takes absolute precedence over reputation; defamation actions are constitutionally prohibited
options.C: Both are constitutional values; courts apply a proportionality approach, giving greater weight to freedom of expression on matters of public interest while protecting individual reputation from unjustified attack
options.D: The Defamation Act 2009 is an EU law measure and is immune from constitutional challenge under Art. 29.4.6°

questions.explanation: Irish law recognises both freedom of expression (Art. 40.6.1°(i)) and the right to one's good name (Art. 40.3.2°) as constitutional values. The Defamation Act 2009 seeks to balance these: it introduces a 'fair and reasonable publication' defence (s.26) for matters of public interest, reflecting the constitutional importance of freedom of expression on public affairs. Courts apply a proportionality analysis when these values conflict, giving greater latitude for expression on matters of genuine public concern.

questions.question: Article 40.3.2° protects the good name of every citizen. A journalist publishes an article making false allegations about a politician's personal life. The politician sues for defamation. The journalist pleads that the article concerned a matter of public interest. Under the Defamation Act 2009, which defence is most relevant?

options.A: Absolute privilege — all political commentary is absolutely privileged
options.B: Justification (truth) — but this fails if the allegations are false
options.C: The defence of fair and reasonable publication on a matter of public interest under s.26 of the Defamation Act 2009
options.D: Qualified privilege under s.18 — available to all journalists regardless of the truth of the publication

questions.explanation: The s.26 Defamation Act 2009 defence of fair and reasonable publication on a matter of public interest is the most relevant. Even where specific allegations are false, the journalist may rely on this defence if the subject matter is of genuine public interest, the journalist acted reasonably in all the circumstances, and publication was fair in the circumstances. The politician's personal life may be relevant to their public role depending on the context. This defence reflects the constitutional balance between Art. 40.6 and Art. 40.3.2°.

questions.question: The Constitution originally provided for criminal punishment for 'blasphemous' publication. The 37th Amendment 2018 removed this provision. What was the previous constitutional position regarding blasphemy?

options.A: Blasphemy was not mentioned in the Constitution prior to 2018 and was only a common law offence
options.B: Article 40.6.1°(i) expressly stated that the publication or utterance of blasphemous matter was an offence punishable by law, and the Defamation Act 2009 had created a blasphemy offence under s.36
options.C: Blasphemy was a constitutional offence only if committed in a public place
options.D: The Constitution required all blasphemy cases to be tried before a Special Criminal Court

questions.explanation: Prior to the 37th Amendment 2018, Art. 40.6.1°(i) expressly stated that the publication or utterance of 'blasphemous, seditious, or indecent matter' was an offence punishable by law. The Defamation Act 2009 s.36 created a specific blasphemy offence (a criminal offence to publish or utter matter grossly abusive or insulting in relation to matters held sacred by any religion). The 37th Amendment (passed by referendum on 26 October 2018) deleted the blasphemy reference from the Constitution; the Defamation (Amendment) Act 2019 then removed the s.36 offence.

questions.question: In McKenna v An Taoiseach (No.2) [1995] 2 IR 10, the Supreme Court imposed constitutional limits on Government use of public funds in referendum campaigns. The constitutional basis was:

options.A: Article 46 which prohibits Government expenditure on referendum campaigns
options.B: The constitutional right to free expression and the right of the people to make an informed decision on a referendum, which the Government violated by spending public funds to advocate a particular outcome
options.C: Article 17.2 which prohibits Government expenditure not authorised by Dáil resolution
options.D: Article 15.2 which vests legislative power in the Oireachtas, not the Government, in respect of referendum campaigns

questions.explanation: McKenna v An Taoiseach (No.2) [1995] 2 IR 10 held that the Government's use of public funds (approximately IR£500,000) to advocate a 'Yes' vote in the divorce referendum was unconstitutional. The Supreme Court held that spending public money to influence voters' free choice in a referendum violated the equality of citizenship (Art. 40.1) and the democratic right to a free, fair and equal vote by improperly distorting the debate through the use of State resources to promote one side.

questions.question: The Broadcasting Act 2009 imposes obligations on broadcasters including requirements for impartiality and balance in news. Does this engage Article 40.6.1°(i)?

options.A: No — broadcasters are not citizens and so have no freedom of expression rights under Art. 40.6.1°(i)
options.B: Yes — statutory obligations of impartiality limit broadcasters’ editorial freedom and engage Art. 40.6.1°(i), but may be justified as proportionate regulations in the public interest given the particular impact of broadcast media on public opinion
options.C: No — broadcast regulation is exclusively an EU competence under the Audiovisual Media Services Directive
options.D: Yes, but only where the statutory obligations relate to political broadcasting immediately before an election

questions.explanation: The Broadcasting Act 2009's impartiality obligations engage Art. 40.6.1°(i) because they constrain broadcasters' expression. However, the Supreme Court in Murphy v IRTC [1999] and in the broader context recognised that broadcast media occupies a special position: it has particular penetration and influence on public opinion, justifying regulatory obligations that would be disproportionate for print media. The constitutionality of impartiality requirements rests on their proportionality — a legitimate aim pursued by appropriate means.

questions.question: Contempt of court can restrict the freedom of the press by prohibiting publication of material relating to ongoing proceedings (sub judice rule). How is this justified under Art. 40.6.1°(i)?

options.A: Contempt of court is absolutely privileged and immune from constitutional challenge
options.B: The sub judice rule is a proportionate restriction justified by the need to protect the constitutional right to a fair trial (Art. 38.1) and the authority of the courts — a limitation expressly contemplated in Art. 40.6
options.C: Contempt restrictions are justified solely on the basis of the common law and need no constitutional analysis
options.D: The sub judice rule applies only to criminal proceedings; civil proceedings enjoy no protection against media comment

questions.explanation: The sub judice rule restricting publication of prejudicial material about ongoing legal proceedings engages Art. 40.6.1°(i) but is constitutionally justified on two grounds: (i) protecting the constitutional right to a fair trial (Art. 38.1) for the accused; and (ii) the 'authority of the State' limitation in Art. 40.6 includes protecting the due administration of justice by the courts. The restriction must be proportionate — overly broad or prolonged restrictions on media reporting could themselves become unconstitutional.

questions.question: A documentary filmmaker seeks to publish a film containing interviews with individuals who were convicted of serious crimes. The victims seek an injunction to prevent broadcast on the basis that it will further damage their dignity. What constitutional analysis applies?

options.A: The documentary is absolutely protected by Art. 40.6.1°(i) and cannot be restrained by any court order
options.B: The court must balance Art. 40.6.1°(i) (freedom of expression) against the Art. 40.3.2° right to good name and Art. 40.3.1° dignity rights of the victims, applying a proportionality test to determine whether prior restraint is justified
options.C: The injunction must be granted automatically as victims’ rights always override media freedom
options.D: The Broadcasting Authority of Ireland must determine the issue first before any court can intervene

questions.explanation: This scenario requires balancing competing constitutional values: Art. 40.6.1°(i) (freedom of expression/documentary filmmaking) against Art. 40.3.2° (right to one’s good name) and Art. 40.3.1° personal rights of the victims. Irish courts apply a proportionality test, recognising that prior restraint is a particularly serious interference with freedom of expression and is only appropriate where the plaintiff shows a clear right to the relief and that damages would be inadequate. The public interest in the documentary's subject matter is a significant factor.

questions.question: Article 40.6.1°(i) protects the expression of 'convictions and opinions'. Does it also protect commercial speech (advertising)?

options.A: Yes — all speech is equally protected by Art. 40.6.1°(i) regardless of its commercial character
options.B: Commercial speech receives protection under Art. 40.6.1°(i) but may be subject to greater State regulation than political or creative speech, given its commercial nature and potential for consumer harm
options.C: No — commercial speech is exclusively regulated by consumer protection legislation and has no constitutional protection
options.D: Commercial speech can only be restricted by Acts of the Oireachtas, not by secondary legislation or regulatory codes

questions.explanation: Irish courts have extended the Art. 40.6.1°(i) protection to commercial expression, including advertising, but have recognised that commercial speech may be subject to more extensive regulation than political or artistic speech. The rationale is that commercial speech is motivated by profit and may create consumer harm (misleading advertising, manipulation) in ways that justify greater State intervention. Regulations on commercial advertising that pursue legitimate consumer protection objectives are more likely to be constitutionally upheld than equivalent restrictions on political speech.

questions.question: In Heaney and McGuinness v Ireland [1994] 3 IR 593, the Supreme Court considered s.52 of the Offences Against the State Act 1939, which required persons to provide an account of their movements and actions under penalty. While addressing Art. 38.1 primarily, the court also applied the proportionality test developed in which earlier case to assess the restriction on freedom of expression and the right to silence?

options.A: Quinn v Ireland (2001) ECHR — the court applied the ECHR proportionality standard
options.B: Heaney v Ireland [1994] 3 IR 593 itself established the Irish proportionality test: the restriction must (i) respond to a pressing social need; (ii) interfere with the right as little as possible; and (iii) maintain a reasonable proportionality between the interference and the objective
options.C: Murphy v IRTC [1999] 1 IR 12 which first articulated proportionality for expression restrictions
options.D: State (Lynch) v Cooney [1982] IR 337 which held all expression restrictions automatically proportionate if authorised by statute

questions.explanation: Heaney v Ireland [1994] 3 IR 593 articulated the Irish constitutional proportionality test: a restriction on a constitutional right is permissible only if it (i) is for a legitimate aim (responds to a pressing social need); (ii) is rationally connected to that aim; (iii) impairs the right as little as reasonably possible; and (iv) maintains a fair balance (proportionality stricto sensu). This test, derived from the approach in Schmidtand Dahlström v Sweden (1976) ECHR and adapted for Irish constitutional law, has become the standard framework for reviewing rights restrictions.

questions.question: The Electoral Acts contain restrictions on third-party spending in election campaigns. Are such restrictions constitutionally permissible under Art. 40.6.1°(i)?

options.A: No — any spending limit restricts political expression and is automatically unconstitutional
options.B: Yes — spending limits are a constitutionally permissible means of ensuring equality in the democratic process by preventing wealthy interests from dominating political communication; they pursue a compelling constitutional objective (fair elections)
options.C: Yes, but only during the formal election period; restrictions at other times violate Art. 40.6.1°(i)
options.D: No — McKenna v An Taoiseach (No.2) [1995] prohibits all State intervention in the content or funding of political speech

questions.explanation: Third-party spending limits in elections are a proportionate restriction on Art. 40.6.1°(i) freedom of expression, justified by the compelling democratic objective of ensuring equality of participation and preventing wealthy interests from distorting elections. The constitutional values of democratic equality (Art. 40.1) and fair elections provide a compelling justification for spending limits, provided they are proportionate. McKenna v An Taoiseach (No.2) [1995] concerned the Government's use of public funds — a different issue from private third-party expenditure limits.

questions.question: Article 40.6.1°(i) states that laws regulating the press 'shall contain no political, religious or class discrimination'. What is the constitutional significance of this anti-discrimination clause for press regulation?

options.A: It means that all press regulation must be approved by a proportional representation electoral system before taking effect
options.B: It prohibits the Oireachtas from enacting press regulation laws that discriminate between different sections of the press based on their political, religious or class orientation — press regulation must be neutral
options.C: It prohibits all State regulation of newspapers regardless of the subject matter
options.D: It means that only the courts, not the Oireachtas, may regulate press content

questions.explanation: The anti-discrimination clause in Art. 40.6.1°(i) ('the education of public opinion being, however, a matter of such grave import to the common good, that the State shall endeavour to ensure that organs of public opinion... shall not be used to undermine public order or morality... The publication or utterance of blasphemous, seditious, or indecent matter is an offence which shall be punishable in accordance with law') is supplemented by the prohibition on political, religious or class discrimination in laws regulating the press. This ensures that press regulation is content-neutral — it cannot target publications because of their political stance, religion or audience class.
